query: effectiveness of dupilumab in asthma treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Contraindications -- Acute bronchospasm, asthma, corticosteroid withdrawal, and status asthmaticus
retrieved_docs.content: While dupilumab is approved as an adjuvant or maintenance therapy to treat eosinophilic and glucocorticoid-dependent moderate-to-severe asthma, it should not be used by itself to treat episodes of asthma exacerbations or acute bronchospasm. [31] Patients with comorbid asthma who start dupilumab therapy for atopic dermatitis or chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis should be advised to stay on their asthma medications to prevent the onset of any adverse event. [31] However, a study by Rabe et al revealed that for patients on oral glucocorticoid therapy for moderate-to-severe asthma or acute asthma exacerbations, adding dupilumab not only led to an overall reduction in the glucocorticoid use but also alleviation of symptoms associated with acute asthmatic episodes or exacerbations over 24 weeks. The only notable but transient adverse effect was reversible eosinophilia in about 14% of the patients. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medications -- Clinical Significance -- Monoclonal Antibody Immune-Modulating Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab, a human monoclonal antibody to the IL-4 receptor, blocks both IL-4 and IL-13 signaling. Wenzel et al. demonstrated a 60% to 80% reduction in exacerbations in asthma patients already on inhaled corticosteroid-LABA combination. The responders did have higher baseline IgE levels. It is yet to be FDA approved for asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Severe Asthma
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with severe eosinophilic asthma who are not adequately controlled can utilize mepolizumab and reslizumab, monoclonal antibodies against IL-5, benralizumab, a monoclonal antibody against the IL-5 receptor α-subunit, and dupilumab a monoclonal antibody against the IL-4 receptor α-subunit. Tezepelumab is a human monoclonal IgG2-λ antibody that binds to TSLP, preventing its interaction with the TSLP receptor complex. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Introduction -- Medications, Biologics, and Immunotherapy for Pediatric Asthma
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-interleukin-4 receptor α monoclonal antibody: Dupilumab is an IgG4 antibody that inhibits IL-4 and IL-13 signaling by binding to the IL-4R α subunit, inhibiting the release of proinflammatory cytokines and chemokines. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab is an effective and safe immunomodulating therapy for treating moderate-to-severe symptoms associated with atopic dermatitis, including itching, poor sleep quality, anxiety, and depression. Evidence from increasing trials suggests that the benefits of dupilumab far outweigh its side effects. Since no concrete literature-based evidence suggests that dupilumab is contraindicated during pregnancy or lactation, shared decision-making among healthcare team members and patients is paramount for ensuring safe and effective treatment plans. Patients should receive significant education on their treatment options, including discussing the benefits and risks to maternal and fetal health, and that patient values and preferences are considered. Consultation with an immunologist is required before initiating dupilumab for severe asthma. Moderate to severe atopic dermatitis is best managed in consultation with a dermatologist. Otorhinolaryngologist consultation is necessary for patients with chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis. Both pharmacists and nurses need easy and direct access to the prescriber so any patient concerns regarding dupilumab use can be addressed immediately. Communication among healthcare team members is pivotal to obtaining optimal therapeutic results for dupilumab use with minimal adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Apply evidence-based guidelines and data to guide dupilumab treatment decisions in clinical practice. Identify the appropriate indications for dupilumab in the treatment of moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis. Assess the treatment response and monitor for adverse effects during dupilumab therapy. Implement effective collaboration among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from dupilumab. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab has been shown to enhance lung function and decrease severe exacerbations in patients with uncontrolled persistent asthma, irrespective of baseline eosinophil count, and when used in addition to inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) and long-acting β-2 agonists (LABA), as compared to standard asthma therapy. [6] [7] The American Thoracic Society (ATS) and European Respiratory Society (ERS) guidelines recommend using dupilumab as an additional treatment option for patients with severe eosinophilic asthma or dependence on oral corticosteroids. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians are advised to monitor asthmatic patients with vasculitic rash, worsening pulmonary symptoms, cardiac complications, and neuropathy in the presence of eosinophilia, as dupilumab use in asthmatics may be associated with eosinophilic pneumonia or vasculitis. [25] Additionally, all healthcare team personnel must actively participate in patient counseling and provide access to resources and contacts if the need to contact the interprofessional team arises. [25] Social workers may not be effective in identifying reasons for lack of treatment adherence. Insurance delays can cause treatment delays. A follow-up by a nurse or medical assistant can help ensure timely medication and positive outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Vernal Keratoconjunctivitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic treatment may be necessary for a few patients with VKC. These may be particularly useful in patients with a history of an associated atopic disease like atopic dermatitis or asthma. Drugs like montelukast, a leukotriene receptor inhibitor, which acts by inhibiting interleukin-10 helpful in treating asthma, have demonstrated benefits by reducing signs and symptoms among VKC patients. [24] Another monoclonal antibody, dupilumab, which acts by inhibiting interleukin-4 receptors, a drug used to treat atopic dermatitis, has also proven beneficial. Omalizumab, another anti-IgE monoclonal antibody, has been used off-label among recalcitrant cases of VKC. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody of the immunoglobulin G4 subclass designed to inhibit receptor signaling downstream of the JAK-STAT pathway by blocking interleukin-4/interleukin-13 (IL-4/IL-13) receptors. [4] As an IL-4Rα antagonist, dupilumab inhibits the signaling of pro-inflammatory cytokines, or interleukins, that induce inflammatory and immunological responses in several conditions, including eczema, asthma, allergic reactions, and rhinosinusitis. [18] Essentially, IL-4Rα is a component common to both IL-4 and IL-13 receptor complexes and is ubiquitously expressed in innate and adaptive immune cells to promote the signaling of IL-4 and IL-13. [19] [20] By blocking this pathway, dupilumab affects 3 integral disease mechanisms of atopic dermatitis: the decrease of skin barrier function, the class switch to IgE, and the Th2 differentiation. [4] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Biological Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Biological agents are possible for children aged 6 and older who have not responded to traditional therapies. The candidates for omalizumab, a monoclonal antibody against IgE, are children with sensitization to at least 1 perennial aeroallergen and moderate-to-severe asthma. Dupilumab, another monoclonal antibody targeting the IL-4 receptor and inhibiting IL-4 and IL-13 signaling, is used in children aged 6 and older as additional maintenance therapy. Mepolizumab, which targets the IL-5 receptor, is utilized in children with severe eosinophilic asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Indications -- Non-FDA Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Non-FDA-approved indications include allergic contact dermatitis, hand dermatitis, chronic spontaneous urticaria, and alopecia areata. [1] [14] Extensive literature suggests the efficacy of dupilumab in treating conditions characterized by Th2-mediated inflammation. [15] Clinical investigations are currently being conducted to assess the effectiveness of dupilumab in treating autoimmune blistering diseases, including bullous pemphigoid and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab is a human monoclonal antibody administered subcutaneously to inhibit the interleukin-4 receptor subunit α (IL-4R α). The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved dupilumab in April 2017 for the treatment of moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis (AD) in adults and pediatric patients aged 6 and older, as well as for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis (CRSwNP) in adults. [1] Since the incidence of atopic dermatitis has been rising, especially in the developed and industrialized parts of the world, dermatologists must prescribe treatment regimens that are both effective and safe to use in the long-term management of this chronic skin condition. [2] Dupilumab is also used in treating adults with atopic dermatitis symptoms inadequately controlled with topical corticosteroid use. [3] [4] Currently, no specific guidelines deem the drug safe for children younger than 6. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab is an injectable prescription medication administered subcutaneously to treat severe and refractory forms of atopic dermatitis. The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved the medication in April 2017 to treat moderate-to-severe, resistant, atopic dermatitis unresponsive to conventional therapy, including topical corticosteroids. Dupilumab is also used as a maintenance treatment for asthma and chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis in adults and children, in conjunction with other medications.

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Patients must be monitored for serious and sudden onset reactions when on dupilumab therapy. It is strongly advised to discontinue dupilumab if a systemic hypersensitivity reaction is noted. [25] Additionally, patients must report to their clinician any worsening eye symptoms leading to conjunctivitis and/or keratitis. Patients with comorbid asthma must be monitored periodically to ensure that they continue their usual asthma treatment. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Following a subcutaneous (SC) dose of dupilumab, the peak plasma concentration (Cmax) is typically achieved about 1 week after the dose. In most clinical trials, the steady-state concentrations were reached by week 16. [22] The bioavailability of dupilumab is similar among patients with atopic dermatitis, asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis (CRSwNP), eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE), and peanut allergy after a SC dose, ranging from 61% to 64%. Distribution: The estimated total volume of distribution is approximately 4.8±1.3 L.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690
retrieved_docs.content: Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab and mepolizumab are both classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” indicating that each of these agents is recognized as a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions such as asthma. This classification groups the two drugs together within the same therapeutic class, highlighting their shared role in managing airway inflammation and improving respiratory outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This ATC category comprises systemic agents that are used to manage obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. By being members of the same pharmacological class, these drugs share a therapeutic purpose of improving airway function, although they achieve this through different mechanisms—benralizumab and mepolizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway, reslizumab also acts on the IL‑5 axis, and amlexanox exerts anti‑inflammatory effects. Recognizing that all four agents belong to this specific ATC group helps clinicians understand their role in the broader treatment strategy for obstructive airway diseases and facilitates appropriate drug selection and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Methapyrilen and omalizumab are both assigned to the therapeutic class of substances that affect the respiratory system. As members of this class, they are used to influence breathing function and are relevant in the treatment of respiratory disorders. Omalizumab, an anti‑IgE monoclonal antibody, is employed in the management of allergic asthma and other IgE‑mediated respiratory conditions, while methapyrilen, a respiratory‑acting agent, is utilized to modify airway dynamics. Recognizing that both drugs belong to the same therapeutic category helps clinicians consider them when selecting pharmacologic strategies aimed at modulating respiratory function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorization indicates that each of these agents is a systemic therapy used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Although they share the same therapeutic class, the drugs differ in their mechanisms of action: amlexanox is a small‑molecule agent, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target interleukin‑5, and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody directed against thymic stromal lymphopoietin. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their common role in managing severe or refractory obstructive airway disease, while highlighting the variety of pharmacologic strategies available within this therapeutic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 697
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” category in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. Amlexanox, although less commonly used for airway disease, is also placed in this category, reflecting its systemic therapeutic role. Grouping these diverse agents together highlights the range of systemic pharmacologic strategies available for managing obstructive airway diseases and underscores their shared classification despite differing mechanisms of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2708
retrieved_docs.content: Albuterol and omalizumab are both members of the therapeutic class described as “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” (substances that affect the respiratory system). This classification indicates that each drug exerts its primary pharmacologic action on the respiratory tract: albuterol functions as a bronchodilator commonly used to relieve bronchospasm in asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, while omalizumab is an anti‑IgE monoclonal antibody employed to control severe allergic asthma and other IgE‑mediated respiratory conditions. Recognizing that both agents belong to the same respiratory‑system‑targeting therapeutic class helps clinicians understand their related mechanisms and consider them within a unified framework for managing airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 116
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox is classified as a member of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” indicating its systemic use in the management of airway obstruction. Within the overarching therapeutic category “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” which encompasses both systemic and inhaled agents, the subclass “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases in ATC” is a child of this broad class and is linked to the larger adrenergics‑for‑systemic‑use group, reflecting that many of its members act on adrenergic pathways. Systemic agents in this subclass include fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast and tezepelumab, representing mechanisms such as non‑selective β‑adrenergic activity (fenspiride), IL‑5‑targeting monoclonal antibodies (benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab), phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibition (roflumilast) and thymic stromal lymphopoietin blockade (tezepelumab) for asthma and related obstructive diseases. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2782
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category encompasses systemic pharmacologic agents used to treat conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). By belonging to the same ATC class, these three drugs share a therapeutic focus on modulating inflammatory pathways or other mechanisms to improve airway function, highlighting their related roles in the management of obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This grouping indicates that each of these agents is a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions, such as severe asthma, and they share a common therapeutic classification within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, eprozinol, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, seratrodast and tezepelumab are members of the ATC subgroup “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” which falls under the broader class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES.” Within this systemic subgroup, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are biologic agents that inhibit interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin pathways; seratrodast is a thromboxane‑A₂ receptor antagonist; eprozinol and fenspiride possess antihistaminic and anti‑inflammatory properties; and amlexanox exerts anti‑inflammatory effects. The ATC hierarchy also contains an inhalant branch. The subclass “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants” includes anti‑inflammatory inhaled corticosteroids, which are organized as child entities: beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, beclomethasone dipropionate (Czech: beklomethason dipropionát) and fluticasone propionate (Czech: flutikason propionát). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, distinguishing them from inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids. By sharing this classification, these agents are recognized as systemic options that target specific inflammatory pathways in severe or refractory disease, helping clinicians select appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1275
retrieved_docs.content: Almitrin, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as agents that affect the respiratory system (“látky ovlivňující dýchací systém”). Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab each belong to this therapeutic class and are further grouped within the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases in ATC”. The therapeutic class “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” itself is a subclass of the broader ATC group “other respiratory system products in ATC”. Together, these classifications indicate that the four biologic drugs are systemic treatments for obstructive airway diseases such as asthma, and they are organized under consistent pharmacological categories that facilitate prescribing, regulation and clinical understanding of respiratory‑targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1595
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” Within this broader ATC category, these agents are also linked to the therapeutic class described in Czech as “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” (substances affecting the respiratory system), which itself is a subset of the larger ATC group “other respiratory system products.” The therapeutic class “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” includes benralizumab, methapyrilen, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab, indicating that these drugs share a common mechanism of action or therapeutic purpose related to modulation of respiratory pathways. Consequently, the classification system reflects that these medications—particularly the monoclonal antibodies benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab, which target interleukin‑5 or its receptor and are used for severe eosinophilic asthma—are grouped together both as systemic treatments for obstructive airway disease and as agents that specifically affect the respiratory system, while amlexanox, although mechanistically different, is also placed in the same ATC subgroup for its role in managing obstructive airway conditions. This integrated categorization aids clinicians in recognizing the shared therapeutic context of these drugs and facilitates appropriate selection and management of treatments for patients with obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2923
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑asthmatic therapy involves the corticosteroid beclomethasone, which is classified as an inhaled steroid agent (ISA) and is used to manage airway disease. Beclomethasone specifically targets inflammation (الالتهاب), reducing inflammatory processes in the respiratory tract and thereby improving asthma control. This highlights beclomethasone’s central role as an anti‑asthmatic medication that treats inflammatory airway conditions.
